title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
subtitle: The Two Systems That Drive the Way We Think
author: Daniel Kahneman
themes: Two distinct cognitive systems — fast/intuitive vs slow/deliberative — govern every human judgment and choice
Loss aversion: the pain of losing is psychologically roughly twice as powerful as the pleasure of an equivalent gain
System 1 generates declarations, beliefs, and impressions without effort; System 2 endorses those outputs with minimal scrutiny
WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is): minds construct confident coherent narratives from limited information without registering what is missing
Prospect theory replaces expected utility theory with a value function defined over changes, not final states, with reference-dependent evaluation
The experienced self and the remembering self have systematically different preferences and make systematically different evaluations of the same events
Expert judgment in low-regularity domains is about as accurate as chance; confidence is decoupled from accuracy
Priming effects and subtle environmental cues influence behavior outside awareness — though scientific reliability of this literature has been seriously challenged
awards: Los Angeles Times Book Prize Current Interest 2011
National Academy of Sciences Communication Award 2012
One of The Economist's Best Books of 2011
One of The Wall Street Journal's Best Nonfiction Books of 2011
One of The New York Times 10 Best Books of 2011
excerpt: Daniel Kahneman's twenty-year synthesis of decades of Nobel-winning research reveals that human judgment is governed by two systems: a fast, intuitive, associative System 1 that runs in the background of consciousness, and a slow, effortful, calculating System 2 that monitors it with consummate laziness. From this architecture flow every major cognitive bias — from anchoring and the conjunction fallacy to loss aversion and the planning fallacy — and the book's great argument is that these biases are not occasional mistakes but structural features of the mind that influence every calculation in finance, law, medicine, and daily life. Embedded in this vast catalogue is prospect theory, the Nobel-winning alternative to centuries of rational-utility economics, which showed that losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains please. The book closes by extending its framework to the deepest question of all: who, between the experiencing self and the remembering self, should we trust to evaluate the quality of a life?
rationale: As the root book (Book #1) for behavioral-finance, this is the indispensable source text for the entire field. Every subsequent work in the leaf directory — from Taleb's contrarian skepticism to Housel's personal finance narrative to Munger's mental-model framework — engages directly with the dual-process architecture and prospect theory that Kahneman established. Without this entry, the behavioral-finance leaf has no conceptual foundation. The book is also the most universally recognized work in the category: its vocabulary (anchoring, loss aversion, System 1/System 2, framing effect) has entered the default professional language of investors, policymakers, and managers.
